---
title: "Final_Urban_Influence"
author: "Dawson Miller"
output: html_document
date: "2025-04-10"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r}
rm(list = ls())




library(readr)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggpubr)
```

## Reading CSV File

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r}
urban <- read.csv("final_urban_data_2.csv")


head(urban)
```

## Average engagement (School vs. Academic)

```{r}


# Prepare the data: long format
urban_long <- urban %>%
  mutate(Student = row_number()) %>%
  select(Student, academic_engagement_total, social_engagement_total) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -Student, names_to = "Engagement_Type", values_to = "Score")

# Rename levels to clean them up
urban_long$Engagement_Type <- recode(urban_long$Engagement_Type,
                                     "academic_engagement_total" = "Academic",
                                     "social_engagement_total" = "Social")

# Plot it
ggplot(urban_long, aes(x = Student, y = Score, color = Engagement_Type)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +  # Use geom_line() instead for connected lines
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "black") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Academic" = "#1f77b4", "Social" = "orange")) +
  labs(title = "Student Engagement: Academic vs Social",
       x = "Student",
       y = "Engagement Score",
       color = "Engagement Type") +
  theme_pubr()

ggsave("Scatter_Plot.png")

```

```{r}
exclude_cols <- c("school_year", "friend_group", "in_person_time", "volunteer",
                  "personal_growth", "home_or_college", "on_campus_job",
                  "campus_jobs_total", "attend_class", "academic_engagement_total", "social_engagement_total")

# Select only the relevant columns
included_data <- urban %>%
  select(-one_of(exclude_cols))

# Compute averages
engagement_averages <- included_data %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), ~mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE))) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(), names_to = "Category", values_to = "Average")

# Rename columns for cleaner plot labels and create the 'Engagement_Type' category
engagement_averages <- engagement_averages %>%
  mutate(Category = recode(Category,
                           social_clubs     = "Social Clubs",
                           religious_groups = "Religious Groups",
                           diversity        = "Diversity & Inclusion",
                           honors           = "Honors Program",
                           res_life         = "Residence Life",
                           study_groups     = "Study Groups",
                           academic_clubs   = "Academic Clubs",
                           cultural_events  = "Cultural Events",
                           rec_im           = "Rec IM"),
         Engagement_Type = case_when(
           Category %in% c("Study Groups", "Academic Clubs", "Honors Program") ~ "Academic",
           Category %in% c("Social Clubs", "Religious Groups", "Diversity & Inclusion", "Residence Life", "Cultural Events", "Rec IM") ~ "Social",
           TRUE ~ NA_character_ # Handle any uncategorized items
         ))

# Plot with color based on Engagement_Type
ggplot(engagement_averages, aes(x = reorder(Category, -Average), y = Average, fill = Engagement_Type)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Academic" = "#1f77b4", "Social" = "orange")) +
  labs(title = "Average Engagement in Clubs & Extra Curriculars",
       x = "Engagement Area",
       y = "Average Score",
       fill = "Engagement Type") + 
  theme_pubr() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

ggsave("Bar_Graph.png")

```

### Chart of Average Engagement in Clubs & Extra Curricular Activities

```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(knitr)

exclude_cols <- c("school_year", "friend_group", "in_person_time", "volunteer",
                  "personal_growth", "home_or_college", "on_campus_job",
                  "campus_jobs_total", "attend_class", "academic_engagement_total", "social_engagement_total")

# Select only the relevant columns
included_data <- urban %>%
  select(-one_of(exclude_cols))

# Compute averages
engagement_averages_table <- included_data %>%
  summarise(across(everything(), ~mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE))) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(), names_to = "Category", values_to = "Average") %>%
  mutate(Category = recode(Category,
                           social_clubs     = "Social Clubs",
                           religious_groups = "Religious Groups",
                           diversity        = "Diversity & Inclusion",
                           honors           = "Honors Program",
                           res_life         = "Residence Life",
                           study_groups     = "Study Groups",
                           academic_clubs   = "Academic Clubs",
                           cultural_events  = "Cultural Events",
                           rec_im           = "Rec IM"
  )) %>%
  arrange(desc(Average)) # Arrange by average for better readability

# Display the table using kable
kable(engagement_averages_table, digits = 2, col.names = c("Engagement Area", "Average Score (1 - 5)"))


```

## School Year correlation to engagement

```{r}


#Group by school_year and calculate means
grouped_data <- urban %>%
  group_by(school_year) %>%
  summarise(
    social_engagement = mean(social_engagement_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    academic_engagement = mean(academic_engagement_total, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

#Reshape to long format for ggplot
long_data <- pivot_longer(grouped_data, 
                          cols = c(social_engagement, academic_engagement), 
                          names_to = "engagement_type", 
                          values_to = "average_score")

#  bar plot
ggplot(long_data, aes(x = factor(school_year), y = average_score, fill = engagement_type)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge") +
  labs(
    title = "Average Social and Academic Engagement by School Year",
    x = "School Year",
    y = "Average Engagement Score",
    fill = "Engagement Type"
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c("1" = "Freshman", "2" = "Sophomore", "3" = "Junior", "4" = "Senior")) +
  theme_minimal()


```

### Chart of Average Social and Academic Engagement by School Year

```{r}
library(knitr)



# Rename rows for clarity
grouped_data$school_year <- recode(grouped_data$school_year,
                                   `1` = "Freshman",
                                   `2` = "Sophomore",
                                   `3` = "Junior",
                                   `4` = "Senior")

# Show table
kable(grouped_data, digits = 2, col.names = c("School Year", "Social Engagement", "Academic Engagement"))

```

## Heat Map of Correlation between Clubs and Groups

```{r}


exclude_cols <- c("school_year", "friend_group", "in_person_time", "volunteer",
                  "personal_growth", "home_or_college", "on_campus_job",
                  "campus_jobs_total", "attend_class", "academic_engagement_total", "social_engagement_total")

# Select only the relevant numerical engagement columns
correlation_data <- urban %>%
  select(-one_of(exclude_cols))

# Calculate the correlation matrix
correlation_matrix <- cor(correlation_data, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

# Convert the correlation matrix to a tidy data frame for ggplot2
tidy_corr_matrix <- as.data.frame(correlation_matrix) %>%
  mutate(Category1 = rownames(.)) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -Category1, names_to = "Category2", values_to = "Correlation")

# Rename categories for better labels (same as before)
tidy_corr_matrix <- tidy_corr_matrix %>%
  mutate(Category1 = recode(Category1,
                           social_clubs     = "Social Clubs",
                           religious_groups = "Religious Groups",
                           diversity        = "Diversity & Inclusion",
                           honors           = "Honors Program",
                           res_life         = "Residence Life",
                           study_groups     = "Study Groups",
                           academic_clubs   = "Academic Clubs",
                           cultural_events  = "Cultural Events",
                           rec_im           = "Rec IM"),
         Category2 = recode(Category2,
                           social_clubs     = "Social Clubs",
                           religious_groups = "Religious Groups",
                           diversity        = "Diversity & Inclusion",
                           honors           = "Honors Program",
                           res_life         = "Residence Life",
                           study_groups     = "Study Groups",
                           academic_clubs   = "Academic Clubs",
                           cultural_events  = "Cultural Events",
                           rec_im           = "Rec IM"))

# Create the heatmap

ggplot(tidy_corr_matrix, aes(x = Category1, y = Category2, fill = Correlation)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "blue", high = "red", mid = "white",
                       midpoint = 0, limit = c(-1, 1), space = "Lab",
                       name = "Correlation") +
  theme_pubr() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
        axis.title.x = element_blank(),
        axis.title.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major = element_blank(),
        panel.border = element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_blank(),
        legend.position = "right",
        plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14, face = "bold")) +
  coord_fixed() +  # Ensure cells are square
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Correlation, 2)), color = "black", size = 3) +
  ggtitle("Correlation Between Student Engagement Groups")




ggsave("heat_map.png")
```

### Heat Map (Social vs Academic)

[This heat-map will allow you to quickly identify which engagement areas tend to move together (positive correlation) or in opposite directions (negative correlation).]{.underline}

-   Color intensity indicates the strength of the correlation (darker colors mean stronger correlations).
-   Color hue indicates the direction of the correlation (red for positive, blue for negative).
-   The numbers inside the tiles are the actual correlation coefficients.

## T - Test Social Engagement

```{r}
# T-test: Social engagement by on-campus job
t.test(social_engagement_total ~ on_campus_job, data = urban)

```

## T - Test Academic Engagement

```{r}

# Make on_campus_job into a binary factor (if it's not already)
urban$on_campus_job <- as.factor(urban$on_campus_job)

# Then run the t-test
t.test(academic_engagement_total ~ on_campus_job, data = urban)

```

## Box Plot

```{r}
# Load necessary libraries


# -----------------------------------------------
# Academic Engagement by School Year
ggplot(urban, aes(x = as.factor(school_year), y = academic_engagement_total)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "#1f77b4", outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(color = "black", size = 0.8, alpha = 0.8, width = 0.2) +
  labs(
    title = "Academic Engagement Across School Year",
    x = "School Year", 
    y = "Academic Engagement"
  ) +
  theme_pubr() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 13))

ggsave("AE_Box.png")

# -----------------------------------------------
# Social Engagement by School Year
ggplot(urban, aes(x = as.factor(school_year), y = social_engagement_total)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "orange", outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(color = "black", size = 0.8, alpha = 0.8, width = 0.2) +
  labs(
    title = "Social Engagement Across School Year",
    x = "School Year", 
    y = "Social Engagement"
  ) +
  theme_pubr() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 13))

ggsave("SE_Box.png")

# -----------------------------------------------
# Combined Engagement Boxplot
urban_long <- urban %>%
  select(social_engagement_total, academic_engagement_total) %>%
  rename(`Social Engagement` = social_engagement_total,
         `Academic Engagement` = academic_engagement_total) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(), names_to = "Engagement", values_to = "Score")

ggplot(urban_long, aes(x = Engagement, y = Score, fill = Engagement)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(color = "black", size = 1, alpha = 0.9, width = 0.2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Academic Engagement" = "#1f77b4",  # Blue
                               "Social Engagement" = "orange")) + # Orange
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(1, 5)) +
  theme_pubr() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(size = 13),
    axis.title.x = element_blank()
  ) +
  ggtitle("Engagement Boxplot: Social vs Academic") +
  ylab("Score (1–5)")

ggsave("Total_Box_Plot.png")

```

## Anova Multiple Analysis

```{r}
# ANOVA for academic engagement across school year
cat("===== ANOVA: Academic Engagement Across School Year =====\n")
anova_model <- aov(academic_engagement_total ~ as.factor(school_year), data = urban)
print(summary(anova_model))

# Spacer line
cat("\n\n")

# ANOVA for social engagement across school year
cat("===== ANOVA: Social Engagement Across School Year =====\n")
anova_model_social <- aov(social_engagement_total ~ as.factor(school_year), data = urban)
print(summary(anova_model_social))




```

### ANOVA Results

#### **Academic Engagement Across School Year**

The ANOVA test examined whether there are significant differences in **academic engagement** across the freshman - senior school years. The output shows:

-   **F(3, 27) = 0.903**,\
-   **p = 0.453**

This p-value is **greater than 0.05**, indicating that **there is no statistically significant difference** in academic engagement between the different school years. In other words, students across Freshman, Sophomore, Junior, and Senior years tend to report similar levels of academic engagement.

#### **Social Engagement Across School Year**

Similarly, the ANOVA test for **social engagement** yielded:

-   **F(3, 27) = 0.69**,\
-   **p = 0.566**

Again, the p-value exceeds the typical threshold of 0.05, meaning there is **no significant difference in social engagement** across the school years. This suggests that students, regardless of class year, engage socially at roughly the same level.

------------------------------------------------------------------------


```{r}
cor(urban)
```
Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.
